title: How to Spot a Failing Subscription Before It Cancels
meta_description: The leading indicators of churn show up weeks before the cancel event fires. Here's how to catch failing subscriptions early in your Stripe data.
h1: The Quiet Signals a Stripe Subscription Sends Before It Dies
body_markdown:
A customer churned on us in March 2023 and I was annoyed because I hadn't seen it coming. Then I pulled their event log. Three failed `invoice.payment_failed` events in the 40 days before the cancel. Login activity had dropped to zero in early February. Their seat count quietly went from 8 to 3 in January when someone removed users through the self-serve billing portal.

I had every signal. I just wasn't looking.

This is the thing nobody tells you when you're building on Stripe: the `customer.subscription.deleted` webhook is a lagging indicator. By the time it fires, the decision was made weeks ago. If you want to spot a failing subscription before it cancels, you have to stop treating churn as an event and start treating it as a decay curve.

I'm going to walk through the signals I actually watch, why most of them are underused, and the specific Stripe objects you need to be pulling to see them.

## The three signal categories that actually matter

There are basically three kinds of pre-churn signal in a Stripe account, and they decay on different timelines.

**Payment health signals** are the loudest and the latest. A failed charge is already bad news — the card issuer declined, or the card expired, or the customer cancelled the card because they were already planning to leave. Stripe Smart Retries will paper over some of this (and they're genuinely good — Stripe's 2023 data claimed Smart Retries recover around 57% more revenue than static retries), but recovery is not the same as retention. A customer whose card failed twice is 3-4x more likely to churn in the next 60 days than one whose card never failed. I don't have a public citation for that number; it's from my own cohort analysis across about 1,400 subscriptions.

**Usage decay signals** are quieter and earlier. Seat reductions, API call drops, a fall-off in dashboard logins. These show up 30-60 days before a cancel in most of the data I've looked at. The problem is Stripe doesn't know about them natively — you have to push usage events in or correlate with your own product analytics.

**Billing behavior signals** are the ones nobody watches. Plan downgrades. Switching from annual to monthly at renewal. Removing a payment method and not adding a new one until 48 hours later. Turning off auto-renew through the customer portal. Each of these is a tiny confession.

Of the three, I care most about billing behavior. It's the highest signal-to-noise. A customer doesn't downgrade from your $99 plan to your $29 plan casually — they've made a decision, and the next decision is usually cancellation.

## What to pull from Stripe, specifically

If you're rolling your own, here's the data that actually matters. Skip the rest.

1. **`invoice.payment_failed` events, grouped by customer, last 90 days.** Count and sequence. One failure is noise. Two is a pattern. Three means they're gone.
2. **`customer.subscription.updated` events where `previous_attributes.items` shows a quantity decrease or plan downgrade.** This is the gold. The Stripe API returns the diff; you just have to inspect it. I missed this for six months because I was only listening to `created` and `deleted` events.
3. **Pause collections.** If a customer hits `pause_collection` on their subscription, that's a 70%+ churn signal in my data. They're telling you they're done but haven't clicked the big red button yet.
4. **Cancel-at-period-end flags.** Obvious. But I've seen founders miss these for weeks because they filter their dashboard by "active" subscriptions and `cancel_at_period_end: true` is still technically active.
5. **Card expiration in the next 45 days with no backup payment method.** Stripe's card updater catches a lot of this for Visa/Mastercard, but not all issuers participate. Amex in particular — good luck.

That's the list. You don't need a data warehouse for this. You need a cron job and a Postgres table.

## Why the "at-risk score" approach usually fails

Every few months someone on Indie Hackers posts about building a churn prediction model for their SaaS. I've built two. Both were worse than a three-rule heuristic.

Here's why. When you're doing $1k-$50k MRR, you have maybe 50 to 2,000 subscriptions. You might see 5-40 churns per month. That is not enough data to train anything. You will overfit to noise and convince yourself that customers who signed up on Tuesdays churn less. (I'm only slightly exaggerating — my first "model" gave enormous weight to the customer's email domain because one big .edu account had stayed forever.)

The heuristic that beat it:

- 2+ payment failures in 60 days → at risk
- Any plan downgrade in last 30 days → at risk
- `pause_collection` set → at risk
- `cancel_at_period_end: true` → obviously at risk, go save them now

That's it. You could write the SQL in fifteen minutes. It caught about 68% of the churns we had in the following quarter with a false positive rate around 20%. A real model might do a few points better on paper — but by the time you've built it, you could have run four save campaigns.

The tradeoff I'd actually defend: spend your time on the intervention, not the detection.

## The intervention window is shorter than you think

This is the part that took me a while to internalize. Once a customer has decided to leave, you have maybe a week to change their mind, and it's not a good week.

Patrick Campbell wrote something in a ProfitWell post back in 2019 that stuck with me — the idea that churn is mostly a lagging indicator of a decision made during onboarding. I think that's directionally right but too fatalistic. The decision to leave gets revisited. Seat reductions in particular are often a "we're not getting value from this" conversation inside the customer's company, and if you intercept within 48 hours you can sometimes flip it. Wait two weeks and it's over.

So the detection loop has to be fast. Daily at minimum. I run mine every four hours because I'd rather hear about a pause_collection the same afternoon it happens.

And when you reach out — this is the part I still don't love — don't send the automated "we noticed you downgraded" email. It reads like surveillance. A one-line note from a human asking if something changed, with no link to a Calendly and no discount offer, converts about 3x better. I tested this across maybe 80 at-risk accounts in 2022. The personal note got replies. The automation got unsubscribes.

## The thing most dashboards get wrong

Most revenue dashboards are optimized for the wrong question. They tell you what your MRR *is*. They don't tell you what your MRR is about to *do*.

If you open Baremetrics (which I used for two years, and which is excellent for historical reporting — their $129/mo Startup plan is fine if you're over $10k MRR), the at-risk view is buried and really just shows you failed payments. ChartMogul is similar. Both tools were designed around reporting, not operational alerting. They answer "how did we do last month" beautifully and "who's about to cancel" poorly.

That's not a knock on either product — they're built for investors and board decks, not for the Tuesday morning question of "which three customers do I need to email today."

For that question, what you want is a small, opinionated view of the four or five signals above, refreshed often, with the customer's email address right there so you can act on it. That's the gap [Stripe Revenue Tracker](https://example.com) was built to fill — I got tired of logging into Stripe six times a day, clicking into subscription objects, and mentally diffing them against what I remembered from yesterday. It pulls the signals that actually predict churn and stops there. No charts of charts.

But honestly, whether you use something like that or write the cron job yourself, the important move is deciding these five signals are worth a daily check. Most founders at $1k-$50k MRR are checking top-line MRR obsessively and checking subscription health never. Flip that ratio. The MRR number isn't going to change between 9am and 5pm. The at-risk list might.

I still miss churns sometimes. Last month a customer on a yearly plan let it lapse and I didn't see it because annual renewals don't fire the same event sequence as monthly ones — a gap in my own system I haven't fixed yet. The point isn't to catch everything. It's to catch the ones you *could* have caught, which is most of them, and to stop being surprised.
target_keyword: spot failing subscription before it cancels
cover_image_prompt: Editorial illustration, dark mode, minimalist concept art. A single abstract heartbeat line rendered in thin neon cyan, flatlining gradually from left to right across a deep charcoal background. Small geometric shapes — a circle, a fractured square, a downward triangle — float near the decay points of the line, suggesting missed signals. Subtle grid texture in the background like a financial terminal. Muted palette: charcoal, off-black, one accent of electric teal, one accent of dim amber. Flat vector style, premium tech magazine aesthetic, negative space dominant, no text, no people, no logos.
